title: Ecosystems — communities, habitats and niches
examTip: AQA uses ecosystem vocabulary precisely. Make sure you know the hierarchy (organism → population → community → ecosystem) and can distinguish between a habitat, a niche, and a community. Competition and interdependence are frequently tested — always explain the mechanism, not just name it.
introTips: Ecology is about relationships — between organisms, and between organisms and their environment. Get the vocabulary right first.
The hierarchy from organism to ecosystem is a favourite starter question. Learn it in order.
Interdependence means every species depends on others — food, shelter, pollination. If one species disappears, others are affected.
intro: An ecosystem is all the living and non-living things in an area, interacting with each other. From a single rockpool to the Amazon rainforest, ecosystems operate on the same principles: organisms occupy specific roles, compete for limited resources, and depend on each other in ways that aren't always obvious until something changes.

keyPoints.heading: The ecological hierarchy
keyPoints.content: Ecology uses precise terms arranged in a hierarchy. An organism is an individual living thing. A population is all the individuals of one species in a given area. A community is all the populations of different species living in the same area. An ecosystem includes the community plus the abiotic (non-living) environment — temperature, light, water, soil type, pH. Habitat is the place where an organism lives (e.g. a pond, a forest floor). A niche is the role an organism plays in its ecosystem — what it eats, what eats it, when it feeds, how it reproduces. No two species can occupy exactly the same niche — if they do, one will outcompete and replace the other.
writeBullets: Organism → population (one species) → community (all species) → ecosystem (community + abiotic environment)
Habitat = the place where an organism lives
Niche = the role an organism plays in its ecosystem
Abiotic factors = non-living: temperature, light, water, soil, pH
Biotic factors = living: food availability, predators, disease, competition
No two species can occupy exactly the same niche — competitive exclusion

keyTerms.def: All the individuals of one species living in a given area at a given time
All the populations of different species living in the same area and interacting with each other
A community of organisms together with the non-living (abiotic) environment they interact with
The specific place where an organism lives within an ecosystem
The role an organism plays in its ecosystem — including what it eats, when it is active, and how it interacts with other species
Non-living factors in an environment (e.g. temperature, light intensity, water availability, soil pH)
Living factors in an environment (e.g. food availability, predators, competitors, pathogens)

keyPoints.memTip: Organism < Population < Community < Ecosystem. Think of it as zoom-out: one individual → all individuals of that species → all species in the area → everything including the non-living environment. Niche = role (not just place — a niche includes WHAT you eat, WHEN you feed, WHO eats you).

keyPoints.heading: Competition — fighting for limited resources
keyPoints.content: Resources in an ecosystem are limited. Intraspecific competition is competition between individuals of the same species for the same resources (food, mates, territory, water, light). This limits population size — when a population grows too large, resources become scarce and population growth slows. Interspecific competition is competition between individuals of different species for similar resources. The better-adapted species will out-compete the other, which may reduce its population size or force it to use a different niche. Animals compete for: food, water, mates, territory. Plants compete for: light, water, mineral ions, space.
writeBullets: Intraspecific competition: same species competing — limits population size
Interspecific competition: different species competing — better adapted species wins
Animals compete for: food, water, mates, territory
Plants compete for: light, water, mineral ions, space
If resources scarce: population growth slows or species is displaced

keyTerms.def: Competition between individuals of the same species for limited resources
Competition between individuals of different species for similar resources

keyPoints.memTip: Intra = within (same species). Inter = between (different species). Animals compete for FWMT: Food, Water, Mates, Territory. Plants compete for LWMS: Light, Water, Mineral ions, Space.

keyPoints.heading: Interdependence — species need each other
keyPoints.content: Interdependence means that species within a community depend on each other — directly or indirectly. Every organism affects others through: food relationships (predator/prey, herbivore/plant), mutualism (both species benefit, e.g. bees and flowers — bees get nectar, flowers get pollinated), competition, and disease. If one species in a community changes in population, it has knock-on effects on other species. For example: if rabbits decrease, foxes that eat rabbits also decrease; plants eaten by rabbits increase. These relationships mean that stable ecosystems depend on biodiversity — the more species there are, the more stable and resilient the ecosystem.
writeBullets: Interdependence: species depend on each other within a community
Types: food relationships, mutualism, competition, disease
Mutualism: both species benefit (e.g. bees + flowers, nitrogen-fixing bacteria + legumes)
If one species changes, knock-on effects ripple through the community
Greater biodiversity = more stable and resilient ecosystem

keyTerms.def: The way in which organisms in a community depend on each other for resources, shelter, reproduction, and other needs
A relationship where two species both benefit (e.g. bees pollinate flowers while gaining nectar)
The variety of different species living in an ecosystem — higher biodiversity = more stable ecosystem

keyPoints.memTip: Interdependence = species don't live in isolation — they need each other. Mutualism = both benefit (bees get food, flowers get pollinated). Predation = one benefits, one suffers. The key exam point is that when one species changes, others are affected — always explain the chain reaction.

tables.title: Abiotic vs biotic factors
rows: Physical and chemical factors in the environment
Temperature, light intensity, water availability, soil pH, wind speed, salinity
Factors from other organisms in the environment
Food availability, predators, competitors, disease-causing pathogens, pollinators

0.question: What is the difference between a community and an ecosystem?
options: A community includes only animals; an ecosystem includes plants too
A community is all the species living in an area; an ecosystem is the community plus the non-living (abiotic) environment
A community is larger than an ecosystem
An ecosystem includes only the abiotic factors; a community includes only biotic factors
0.correctFeedback: ✅ Exactly — the community is all the living species; the ecosystem adds the non-living environment to that.
0.wrongFeedback: A community is all the populations of different species living in the same area. An ecosystem is the community PLUS the abiotic (non-living) environment — temperature, light, water, soil. The ecosystem is the more complete, broader concept.

1.question: What does an organism's niche describe?
options: Only the physical location where the organism lives
The organism's role in its ecosystem — including what it eats, what eats it, and when it is active
The number of individuals of that species in the ecosystem
The climate and weather conditions in the organism's habitat
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — a niche is more than just a location. It's the organism's full role: its diet, predators, activity patterns, and how it interacts with other species.
1.wrongFeedback: A niche is not just a location — that's a habitat. A niche describes the full role of the organism in its ecosystem: what it eats, what eats it, when it is active, how it reproduces, how it affects the abiotic environment. No two species can occupy exactly the same niche — the better competitor will exclude the other.

commonMistakes: Confusing habitat and niche — habitat is WHERE an organism lives (pond, forest floor). Niche is the ROLE it plays (what it eats, when it feeds, what eats it). A habitat can contain many niches.
Confusing community and ecosystem — community = all the species (biotic only). Ecosystem = community + abiotic environment. The ecosystem is the bigger concept.
Forgetting that plants compete too — plant competition is a common AQA question. Plants compete for light, water, mineral ions, and space — not food.
Saying 'species are interdependent for survival' without explaining how — always give a specific mechanism: food relationship, pollination, nutrient cycling, etc.
examTips: Learn the hierarchy in order: organism → population → community → ecosystem. AQA tests each level's definition and the differences between them.

For interdependence questions, name the specific relationship type and explain the mechanism, e.g. 'bees and flowers have a mutualistic relationship — bees gain nectar for energy while flowers are pollinated, enabling reproduction'.
When asked about competition, state what species are competing FOR, not just that they compete.
Abiotic vs biotic: if it's non-living (temperature, light, water, soil), it's abiotic. If it involves another organism (predators, food, disease), it's biotic.
revisionCardBullets: Habitat = where an organism lives; Niche = its role in the ecosystem
Abiotic factors: temperature, light, water, soil pH (non-living)
Biotic factors: food, predators, competitors, disease (living)
Intraspecific competition: same species — limits population size
Interspecific competition: different species — better adapted wins
Animals compete for: food, water, mates, territory
Plants compete for: light, water, mineral ions, space
Interdependence: species depend on each other — knock-on effects when one changes
Mutualism: both species benefit (e.g. bees + flowers)
